posts.text: Two oil tankers and two LPG carriers are crossing the Strait of Hormuz **inbound** via the Omani route with their AIS turned on (under heavy US Navy / Airforce watch). Washington trying hard to keep the Omani route on.
posts.summary: U.S. authorities are actively maintaining the Omani shipping route as an alternative to the main Strait of Hormuz passage, with several energy tankers currently transiting under close military monitoring to demonstrate the corridor's viability. The move suggests efforts to preserve critical oil and LPG supply routes amid potential disruptions to regional shipping.

posts.text:
Oil and Gas Investment and Field Decline Rates

Global oil and gas producers need to invest more than $500 billion each year just to sustain output in the face of natural declines from existing fields as pressure falls, according to estimates prepared by the International Energy https://t.co/67gXySweOJ
posts.summary: Global oil and gas producers must spend over $500 billion annually to maintain current output levels as existing fields naturally decline, according to IEA estimates. This substantial investment requirement underscores the ongoing capital intensity of sustaining oil and gas supply in the face of reservoir depletion.

posts.text:
Global energy spending is on track to reach $3.4 trillion in 2026

Around $2.2 trillion of this is set to go to grids, storage, low-emissions fuels, nuclear, renewables, efficiency & electrification

And about $1.2 trillion goes to oil, natural gas & coal: https://t.co/8IajZj1gIL https://t.co/yLRuHSwqq9
posts.summary: According to the IEA, approximately $1.2 trillion of the projected $3.4 trillion global energy spending in 2026 will go toward oil, natural gas, and coal, while the remaining $2.2 trillion is directed toward clean energy technologies and infrastructure. This indicates that fossil fuels, including oil, will represent roughly one-third of total global energy investment by 2026.
